title: Project setup: ROS2 package scaffold and launch infrastructure
body:
## Description
Create the foundational ROS2 package structure that all nodes will build on. This includes the package manifest, launch files, parameter configs, and shared utilities (e.g. TF helpers, common constants).

## Technical suggestions
- Use `ros2 pkg create` to scaffold the package with `ament_cmake` or `ament_python`
- Create a top-level `launch/` directory with a `snc.launch.py` that starts all nodes together
- Define a `config/params.yaml` for tunable parameters (e.g. marker confidence threshold, waypoint spacing)
- Add a `requirements.txt` / `package.xml` listing all external dependencies: `nav2_*`, `find_object_2d`, `slam_toolbox`, etc.
- Set up a `README.md` documenting how to build and launch

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Package builds cleanly with `colcon build`
- [ ] `snc.launch.py` exists and launches without errors (even if nodes are stubs)
- [ ] All external package dependencies declared in `package.xml`
- [ ] `config/params.yaml` exists with at least placeholder parameters
- [ ] README documents build and launch steps

title: Node 1: Start marker detection to trigger exploration
body:
## Description
Detect the ArUco/visual start marker shown to the camera and publish a trigger to begin the Search & Navigation Challenge. Also subscribe to `/trigger_start` as a contingency fallback.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `find_object_2d` or OpenCV ArUco detection on the camera topic (`/camera/color/image_raw`)
- On detection, publish to an internal topic (e.g. `/snc_start`) to signal Node 1's navigation logic
- Subscribe to `/trigger_start` (`std_msgs/Empty`) and treat receipt as equivalent to visual detection
- Guard against repeated triggers — only start once
- Update `/snc_status` with a `'waiting for start'` → `'exploring'` state transition

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot begins exploration when start marker is held in front of camera
- [ ] Robot begins exploration when `ros2 topic pub /trigger_start std_msgs/msg/Empty` is run
- [ ] Status topic reflects the state change
- [ ] No duplicate triggers cause erratic behaviour

title: Node 1: Autonomous exploration strategy
body:
## Description
Implement the core autonomous exploration algorithm that drives the ROSBot around the unknown closed space until all hazard markers are found or the 4-minute timeout elapses.

## Technical suggestions
- Choose one primary approach and extend it with custom logic:
  - **Frontier exploration**: parse `/map` (`nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`) to find frontier cells (known free adjacent to unknown), select a target, and send a Nav2 `NavigateToPose` goal
  - **Explore Lite**: integrate the `explore_lite` package but add custom stopping/spinning behaviour
  - **Wall following**: adapt the TheConstruct wall-following example with Nav2 or direct `/cmd_vel` commands
- Periodically stop and spin in place to maximise camera coverage for marker detection
- Subscribe to `/hazards` count; stop exploring once 5 markers are confirmed
- Subscribe to `/trigger_teleop` (`std_msgs/Empty`) — on receipt, suspend autonomous movement so a teleop node can take over
- Publish exploration status to `/snc_status`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot explores the space without collisions (laser-based obstacle avoidance active)
- [ ] Robot stops and spins at intervals to scan for markers
- [ ] Exploration terminates automatically when 5 markers are published on `/hazards`
- [ ] `/trigger_teleop` suspends autonomous movement
- [ ] Custom logic beyond a stock algorithm is demonstrable (e.g. spinning, frontier filtering, marker-count stopping)

title: Node 2: Hazard marker detection via find_object_2d
body:
## Description
Integrate `find_object_2d` to detect hazard markers in the camera image and determine which of the 13 defined marker types has been seen.

## Technical suggestions
- Launch `find_object_2d` with the hazard marker image set from Canvas as training data
- Subscribe to `find_object_2d`'s output topic (typically `/objects`) which provides object ID and 2D bounding-box homography
- Map `find_object_2d` object IDs to the SNC hazard marker IDs (0–12) defined in the spec
- Apply a confidence/size threshold to reject false positives
- Publish detection events to an internal topic for Node 2's localisation pipeline

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All 13 marker types loaded as training images into `find_object_2d`
- [ ] Correct marker ID (per spec table) published on detection
- [ ] False positive rate is low in a static test scene
- [ ] Detection events are debounced (same marker not re-published every frame)

title: Node 2: Hazard marker localisation using secondary sensor
body:
## Description
Compute the map-frame 3D/2D position of each detected hazard marker using a secondary sensor (laser, depth image, or IR range) combined with the TF transform tree.

## Technical suggestions
- **Laser approach**: when a marker is detected, extract the bounding-box centre column, map it to a laser scan angle, and read the corresponding range from `/scan`. Use `tf2` to transform from `laser_frame` → `map` frame.
- **Depth image approach**: back-project the bounding-box centre pixel using the depth image (`/camera/depth/image_raw`) and camera intrinsics, then transform the 3D point from `camera_frame` → `map` via TF.
- **IR range approach**: use the front-facing IR sensors as a distance estimate, combined with robot heading from TF.
- Average several readings over a short window to reduce noise before publishing
- Construct a `visualization_msgs/Marker` with the correct `id` field and `frame_id: "map"`
- Publish to `/hazards`; do not publish the start marker on this topic

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Marker position in `/hazards` is within ~30 cm of the true position in a controlled test
- [ ] `frame_id` is set to `"map"`
- [ ] Marker `id` matches the spec table
- [ ] Secondary sensor data (not just camera) is demonstrably used in position computation
- [ ] Start marker is never published to `/hazards`

title: Node 3: Robot path tracking during exploration
body:
## Description
Periodically record the robot's map-frame pose during exploration and publish the accumulated path on `/path_explore`.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `tf2_ros.Buffer.lookup_transform('map', 'base_link', rclpy.time.Time())` on a timer to get current pose
- Store poses in a list; choose a sampling interval that balances fidelity vs. path density (suggested: 0.2–0.5 s or every ~5 cm of movement)
- Publish as `nav_msgs/Path` on `/path_explore` with `header.frame_id = 'map'`
- Expose the path list to Node 3's return-home logic
- Stop recording when exploration ends and return-home begins

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/path_explore` updates continuously during exploration
- [ ] Path is in the `map` frame
- [ ] Path is smooth and reflects actual robot movement (no large gaps or duplicates)
- [ ] Recording stops cleanly when triggered to return home

title: Node 3: Return-to-home by retracing exploration path
body:
## Description
On completion of exploration (automatic or via `/trigger_home`), navigate the robot back to the start position by following the recorded exploration path in reverse.

## Technical suggestions
- Reverse the stored pose list from Node 3's path tracker
- Thin the waypoint list to remove redundant intermediate points (e.g. keep every Nth point or use a Douglas-Peucker-style filter) — too many waypoints causes Nav2 to thrash
- Choose a return approach:
  - **Nav2 waypoint follower**: send the waypoint list to `nav2_msgs/action/FollowWaypoints`
  - **Sequential single goals**: iterate through reversed waypoints, sending one `NavigateToPose` goal at a time and waiting for completion
  - **Direct `/cmd_vel` PID**: compute heading + distance to each waypoint and drive directly
- Subscribe to `/trigger_home` (`std_msgs/Empty`) to allow manual invocation
- Stop and hold position for ≥5 s once the start position is reached
- Update `/snc_status` throughout

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot returns to start by following (approximate) reverse of exploration path, not a direct straight line
- [ ] `/trigger_home` manually triggers return when not automatic
- [ ] Robot stops for ≥5 s at the home position
- [ ] Return journey begins within a few seconds of exploration ending

title: Node 3: Publish return-home path on /path_return
body:
## Description
Track and publish the robot's path during the return-to-home phase on a separate topic `/path_return`.

## Technical suggestions
- Reuse the same TF-based pose sampling logic from the exploration path tracker
- Begin recording when return-home is triggered; stop when robot halts at home
- Publish as `nav_msgs/Path` on `/path_return` with `header.frame_id = 'map'`
- Both `/path_explore` and `/path_return` should be visible simultaneously in RViz/Foxglove during the return phase

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/path_return` is published and updates during the return phase
- [ ] Path is in the `map` frame
- [ ] `/path_explore` and `/path_return` are distinct and both visible in RViz

title: SLAM and map publishing setup
body:
## Description
Configure and launch SLAM Toolbox (or equivalent) to build and publish an occupancy grid map on `/map` throughout the challenge.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `slam_toolbox` in online async mode: `slam_toolbox/launch/online_async_launch.py`
- Provide a tuned `mapper_params_online_async.yaml` (adjust resolution, scan matching params for the ROSBot's laser)
- Verify the map is published on `/map` as `nav_msgs/OccupancyGrid`
- Ensure TF tree is complete: `map` → `odom` → `base_link` → sensor frames
- Test map quality in simulation before deploying on hardware

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/map` is published and updates in real time during robot movement
- [ ] Map accurately reflects walls and obstacles in a test environment
- [ ] TF tree has no gaps or extrapolation errors
- [ ] SLAM launches as part of the main `snc.launch.py`

title: Nav2 stack configuration and bringup
body:
## Description
Configure and integrate Nav2 for obstacle-aware point-to-point navigation used by the exploration and return-home nodes.

## Technical suggestions
- Use `nav2_bringup` with a custom `nav2_params.yaml` tuned for the ROSBot (footprint, costmap inflation radius, planner speeds)
- Configure the global and local costmaps to use `/map` and `/scan`
- Confirm `NavigateToPose` action server is available and reachable from Node 1 and Node 3
- Test recovery behaviours (spin, back-up) so the robot can self-recover from getting stuck
- Include Nav2 launch in `snc.launch.py`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Robot navigates to a specified pose without hitting obstacles in a test run
- [ ] Nav2 action server is reachable from Python action clients
- [ ] Recovery behaviours activate when robot is stuck
- [ ] Nav2 launches as part of `snc.launch.py`

title: Status topic implementation (/snc_status)
body:
## Description
Implement a shared status publisher so the robot's current operational state is always visible on `/snc_status` throughout the challenge.

## Technical suggestions
- Publish `std_msgs/String` to `/snc_status` at ~1 Hz from whichever node owns the current phase
- Define clear state strings, e.g.: `'waiting for start'`, `'exploring'`, `'marker detected: explosive'`, `'returning home'`, `'challenge complete'`
- Consider a simple state machine (enum or string constant set) shared across nodes via a common module
- Ensure status updates are visible in Foxglove/RViz during the challenge demo

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `/snc_status` publishes continuously throughout the challenge
- [ ] Status string changes meaningfully at each phase transition
- [ ] Marker detections are reflected in status messages
- [ ] Status is human-readable for the assessor

title: Integration testing in simulation
body:
## Description
Run the full software stack end-to-end in a simulated environment (TheConstruct or Gazebo) and verify all topics publish correct data before moving to the real robot.

## Technical suggestions
- Set up a Gazebo world with walls, obstacles, and printed hazard marker textures
- Record a ROS2 bag (`ros2 bag record`) covering `/map`, `/hazards`, `/path_explore`, `/path_return`, `/snc_status` during a full simulated run
- Check marker IDs, frame IDs, and coordinate accuracy by inspecting the bag in RViz
- Document any sim-to-real discrepancies (laser noise, camera exposure) for tuning on hardware

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Full challenge completes in simulation without manual intervention
- [ ] All 5 required topics publish valid data throughout the run
- [ ] ROS2 bag recorded for a complete simulated run and reviewed
- [ ] Known sim-to-real gaps are documented in the repo

title: Real-robot testing and parameter tuning
body:
## Description
Deploy and test the full software stack on the physical ROSBot in the AIIL/Cyber Security Lab and tune parameters for real-world performance.

## Technical suggestions
- Start with a small known environment before the unknown challenge space
- Tune SLAM resolution and Nav2 costmap inflation for the physical laser's noise characteristics
- Adjust `find_object_2d` confidence thresholds for real lighting conditions
- Test and tune waypoint density for return-home (too many → Nav2 thrash; too few → path not followed)
- Verify TF accuracy: check that published hazard marker positions match physical marker locations
- Charge ROSBots after each session; log any hardware issues on Teams/BitBucket

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Full challenge attempted on the real robot at least twice before demo day
- [ ] Hazard marker placement error documented (measured vs. published position)
- [ ] Return-to-home path visually follows exploration path on the real robot
- [ ] Hardware issues logged and reported appropriately

title: Evaluation report: collect evidence throughout development
body:
## Description
Systematically collect video and data evidence throughout the project to support the evaluation report, rather than scrambling at the end.

## Technical suggestions
- Record short screen-capture videos of RViz/Foxglove showing: map building, marker detection with marker IDs visible, path overlays, return-home trajectory
- Save ROS2 bag files from notable test runs (simulation and real-robot)
- Keep a running log of limitations encountered and how they were addressed
- Screenshot terminal output only when it's concise and clearly labelled — prefer RViz visuals
- Track AI tool usage in real time (export Claude/ChatGPT logs as you go)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] At least 3 video recordings of real-robot test runs captured before demo day
- [ ] ROS2 bag file from at least one successful near-complete run saved
- [ ] AI tool usage log maintained and exportable
- [ ] Evidence folder committed to repo with a brief index

title: Evaluation report: write and submit PDF
body:
## Description
Compile all evidence and analysis into a ≤5-page PDF evaluation report, due 11:59 pm Sunday 19 April 2026.

## Technical suggestions
- Structure: (1) brief node summaries + package overview, (2) dependencies/launch/config description, (3) performance analysis with evidence, (4) strengths, (5) limitations, (6) references + AI log summary
- All claims must be backed by specific evidence (video timestamp, bag file screenshot, measured numbers)
- List all ROS2 packages used (`slam_toolbox`, `nav2_*`, `find_object_2d`, etc.) with version/source
- Upload accompanying videos to the MS Teams location specified on Canvas before the deadline
- Silence period begins 5:00 pm Friday 17 April — submit questions before then

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] PDF is ≤5 pages
- [ ] Every performance claim is supported by referenced evidence
- [ ] All dependent packages listed
- [ ] Launch files, config files, and any ML training data described
- [ ] AI tool usage log included or summarised
- [ ] Videos uploaded to MS Teams before deadline
- [ ] PDF submitted via Canvas before 11:59 pm 19 April 2026
